Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Submitt His Claims To Individual Arbitration
Off Calendar Jan-23-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: JOHN HURNEY, ESQ., 818.992.9999, FOR DEFENDANT QUALITY STAFFING LLC; No other appearances.

RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT QUALITY STAFFING LLC's Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Submit His Claims To Individual Arbitration is granted. Pursuant to the arbitration clause of the employment agreement between Plaintiff and Defendant, Plaintiff shall submit his individual claims to arbitration. This action is stayed pending the completion of arbitration. Under the Federal Arbitration Act, which governs the arbitration clause at issue here, "the question whether an arbitration agreement permits classwide arbitration is a gateway matter, which is reserved for judicial determination unless the parties clearly and unmistakenly provide otherwise." (Reed Elsevier, Inc. v. Crockett ((6th Cir. 2013) 734 F.3d 594, 599.) The arbitration clause at issue here does not permit classwide arbitration. "A party may not be compelled under the FAA to submit to class arbitration unless there is a contractual basis for concluding that the party agreed to do so." (Stolt-Nielsen S.A. v. AnimalFeeds International Corp., (2010) 559 U.S. 662, 684; see also Reed Elsevier, Inc. v. Crockett (6th Cir. 2013) 734 F.3d 594, 600, "the agreement does not expressly exclude the possibility of classwide arbitration, which is true enough. But the agreement does not include it either, which is what the agreement needs to do in order for us to force that momentous consequence [of class arbitration] upon the parties here.") The plain language of the arbitration clause indicates that the agreement only contemplated two parties: (1) the agreement is only signed by Chan and Quality Staffing; (2) it only references a "dispute or claim between any employee and the Company'," and therefore does not discuss disputes of multiple employees; (3) it provides a system where one "employee and the Company will select an arbitrator"; (4) it provides no system for multiple employees to come together to choose an arbitrator; (5) it provides the arbitration will be held "in the county and state where the employee works or worked" and does not provide for multiple employees working in multiple counties; and (6) it notes the arbitrator "shall have the authority to require either party to pay the costs and attorneys' fees of the other party," which shows that only two parties were intended, by the use of the words "either" and "other party." Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest Goldsmith, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR5976
